title: Constrain scheduler/core.py to loop, selection, conflicts, and leases
description: Finish the source split by leaving `scheduler/core.py` responsible only for the `Scheduler` core loop, bead selection, conflict detection, and lease management, wiring imports to the extracted modules and checking module-size targets.
acceptance_criteria: `src/agent_takt/scheduler/core.py` contains only the intended core-loop responsibilities.
No scheduler package module exceeds 500 lines.
The scheduler package has no circular imports and preserves the original public API.

handoff_summary.completed: Extracted corrective bead evaluation logic from Scheduler._reevaluate_blocked into BeadExecutor.reevaluate_corrective_state(). Removed dead _find_corrective_child from core.py. Removed unused BEAD_DONE import from core.py. Added BEAD_READY to execution.py imports. All scheduler modules verified under 500 lines; no circular imports; public API intact.
handoff_summary.risks: The corrective evaluation logic was moved from Scheduler._reevaluate_blocked into BeadExecutor.reevaluate_corrective_state without functional change — the same FollowupManager private methods are called, just from one level closer. Tester should verify the full corrective lifecycle: blocked → corrective created → parent requeued → escalation after max attempts.
handoff_summary.next_action: Run tester bead against scheduler/core.py and scheduler/execution.py changes
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: No file-scope conflicts expected; changes are isolated to scheduler package internals.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Corrective bead evaluation was extracted from Scheduler._reevaluate_blocked into a new BeadExecutor.reevaluate_corrective_state() method. This eliminates the self._executor._followups._* private-method chain in core.py, keeping core.py focused on the orchestration loop while BeadExecutor owns the corrective state machine. Alternative considered: moving logic into FollowupManager directly — rejected because FollowupManager doesn't hold storage.update_bead access and BeadExecutor is the natural boundary between the scheduler loop and bead execution concerns. BEAD_DONE import removed from core.py (unused after refactor); BEAD_READY added to execution.py (needed by the new method).
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Tester should verify: (1) blocked bead with open corrective child stays blocked (no double-action); (2) blocked bead with done corrective child gets requeued correctly; (3) already-retried detection prevents infinite requeue loops; (4) escalation fires after max_corrective_attempts; (5) transient-error re-queue path in _reevaluate_blocked still fires before corrective evaluation; (6) full public API import path works unchanged.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: FollowupManager methods accessed by reevaluate_corrective_state are still prefixed with _ (private by convention). A follow-up refactor could promote them to a formal public API, but that is out of scope for this bead per the spec's extract-don't-rewrite principle.

conflict_risks: No file-scope conflicts expected; changes are isolated to scheduler package internals.

execution_history.summary: Constrained scheduler/core.py to core-loop responsibilities by (1) extracting the corrective bead evaluation logic from _reevaluate_blocked into a new BeadExecutor.reevaluate_corrective_state() method in execution.py — eliminating the self._executor._followups._* private method chains in core.py, (2) removing the dead _find_corrective_child method, and (3) removing the now-unused BEAD_DONE import. All modules remain under 500 lines; public API and imports verified clean.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
